_note: Edit the 'facts' and 'hashtags' fields for each road. Do not change 'id', 'osm_name', or 'osm_ref' as these are used to fetch road data from the map.

expressways.name: Ayer Rajah Expressway
facts: The Ayer Rajah Expressway (AYE) stretches approximately 40km across Singapore's southern corridor, connecting Jurong in the west to the city centre.
It passes through some of Singapore's most significant industrial and educational zones, including the National University of Singapore and one-north.
The AYE was progressively built in stages from 1988, making it one of Singapore's longer expressway construction projects.

expressways.name: Bukit Timah Expressway
facts: The Bukit Timah Expressway (BKE) is one of Singapore's shorter expressways at approximately 11km, running north from the PIE junction toward Woodlands.
It passes alongside the Bukit Timah Nature Reserve, one of the last remaining primary rainforest patches in Singapore.
Opened in 1986, the BKE provides a key corridor linking the central region to the causeway and Johor Bahru.

expressways.name: Central Expressway
facts: The Central Expressway (CTE) is Singapore's primary north-south spine, running approximately 17km from Toa Payoh in the north through the city centre.
It includes Singapore's longest road tunnel, passing beneath the Bukit Timah area and the Singapore River district.
The CTE is notorious for being one of Singapore's most congested expressways during peak hours, particularly at its city-bound tunnels.

expressways.name: East Coast Parkway
facts: The East Coast Parkway (ECP) was Singapore's first expressway, opened in 1981 and running approximately 19km along the southern coastline.
Much of the land beneath the ECP is reclaimed — the shoreline here did not exist before the 1970s land reclamation programme.
The ECP connects the CBD to Changi Airport and passes alongside East Coast Park, one of Singapore's most popular recreational corridors.

expressways.name: Kranji Expressway
facts: The Kranji Expressway (KJE) is Singapore's shortest expressway at approximately 9km, connecting Choa Chu Kang to Woodlands and the BKE.
It passes through the northwestern corridor of Singapore, serving new towns like Bukit Panjang and Choa Chu Kang.
Opened in 1994, the KJE was built to support rapid population growth in Singapore's western new towns during the 1990s.

expressways.name: Kallang–Paya Lebar Expressway
facts: The Kallang–Paya Lebar Expressway (KPE) opened in 2008 and includes the world's longest underground expressway tunnel at the time of its completion.
At approximately 12km in total, around 9km of the KPE runs underground, making it a significant feat of tunnelling engineering in Singapore.
The KPE connects the eastern regions of Singapore to the city, providing relief to the heavily trafficked ECP and CTE corridors.

expressways.name: Marina Coastal Expressway
facts: The Marina Coastal Expressway (MCE) is Singapore's newest and shortest expressway, opened in 2013 at approximately 5km in length.
It runs almost entirely underground, connecting the ECP to the AYE beneath the Marina Bay reclaimed land area.
The MCE completed Singapore's expressway ring road system, closing the final gap in the city's highway network.

expressways.name: Pan Island Expressway
facts: The Pan Island Expressway (PIE) is Singapore's longest expressway at approximately 42km, stretching from Tuas in the west to Changi Airport in the east.
Construction began in 1966, making it one of the earliest expressways built as Singapore rapidly developed its road infrastructure.
The PIE connects virtually every other expressway in Singapore, making it the backbone of the island's highway network.

expressways.name: Seletar Expressway
facts: The Seletar Expressway (SLE) runs approximately 13km through Singapore's north, connecting Woodlands to the TPE and the northeastern regions.
It passes close to Seletar Airport, Singapore's general aviation airport and one of the oldest airfields in Southeast Asia.
The SLE opened in 1994 and forms a key part of the northern expressway loop alongside the BKE and TPE.

expressways.name: Tampines Expressway
facts: The Tampines Expressway (TPE) stretches approximately 15km through Singapore's northeastern corridor, connecting the SLE to Changi.
It serves the large residential towns of Sengkang, Punggol, and Tampines, some of Singapore's most densely populated new towns.
The TPE opened in 1991 and has become a critical artery as the northeastern region of Singapore has grown significantly in population.
